content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Peter Ostroff 
Planning Commissioner 
1234 Lago Vista Drive 
Beverly Hills, CA. 90210 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-17-290 
Dear Mr. Ostroff: 
January 10, 2018 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions 
of the Political Reform Act ("Act").1 Please note that we are only providing advice under the Act 
and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions, such as common law conflict of interest 
or Section 1090. 
Also, note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 
FPPC Ops. 71); any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not 
the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for 
additional advice. 
QUESTION 
Do you have a disqualifying conflict of interest from participating in Planning Commission 
decisions to modify the Beverly Hills Municipal Code affecting single family residences when your 
residence is in an area of the city in which future development will be affected? 
CONCLUSION 
Yes, the governmental decisions may foreseeably and materially affect the value of your 
home. However, the proposed modifications to the definition of the term "basement," which apply 
to the entire city, may be properly segmented. 
FACTS 
You were installed as a member of the Beverly Hills Planning Commission on January 1. 
The Planning Commission will be considering proposed modifications by way of ordinances to the 
City's Municipal Code. 
1 The Political Refonn Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


The proposed modifications include: 
File No. A-17-290 
Page No. 2 
• A change in the definition of the tenn ''basement." This change will apply city-wide and 
will make the definition more restrictive than the current provision. 
• Changes to the height of buildings and the height and number of walls, fences, hedges, and 
allowable landfonn alteration to the "Hillside Area" of the city. These changes will be more 
restrictive than current provisions. 
• A reduction in the amount of earth material exported or imported in the Hillside Area that 
will be allowed without a special pennit. 
You are a resident of the Hillside Area, which is a low density, single-family zoning 
designation that has uniform development standards for its residents. Your house was constructed 
approximately two years ago, and it does not have a basement. You do not envision remodeling 
your home within the next few years. 
Your home is currently in compliance with the proposed modification of the Hillside Area 
ordinances. You stated that the potential land to develop has been maximized, so further 
development of your property is not contemplated nor conceivable in your opinion. As your home 
is nuzzled at the bottom of a hill in a densely-forested area, it was designed with a focus on privacy 
rather than maximizing views. 
ANALYSIS 
Under Section 87100, a conflict of interest exists whenever a public official makes, 
participates in making, or uses his or her position to influence a governmental decision that has a 
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on one or more of the official's interests. 
Section 87103(b) indicates that an official has an interest in "[a]ny real property in which 
the public official has a direct or indirect interest of at least $2,000." Your real property interest in 
your residence is the only interest implicated by the decisions. Accordingly, we must determine if 
the decisions will have a foreseeable and material effect. 
Foreseeability: 
The standard for foreseeability differs depending on whether an interest is explicitly 
involved in the decisions. (Regulation 18701 .) An interest is explicitly involved in a decision if the 
interest is a named party in, or the subject of, the governmental decision. (Regulation 18701 (a).) 
Under the facts you have provided, your home is not explicitly involved in the decisions. Thus, 
Regulation 1870l(b) provides: "[i]n general, if the financial effect can be recognized as a realistic 
possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical, it is reasonably foreseeable."2 
2 Regulation l 870l(b) also provides a non-exhaustive list of six factors that may be considered in the 
"reasonably foreseeable" determination. 


Materiality: 
File No. A-17-290 
Page No. 3 
In relevant part, Regulation 18702.2(a) provides that the reasonably foreseeable financial 
effect of a governmental decision on a public official's interest in real property is material when the 
decision: 
"(7) Would change the development potential of the parcel of real property; 
*** 
"( I 0) Would change the character of the parcel of real property by substantially altering 
traffic levels or intensity of use, including parking, of property surrounding the official's real 
property parcel, the view, privacy, noise levels, or air quality, including odors, or any other factors 
that would affect the market value of the real property parcel in which the official has a financial 
interest; 
"( 11) Would consider any decision affecting real property value located within 500 feet of 
the property line of the official's real property, other than commercial property containing a 
business entity where the materiality standards are analyzed under Regulation 18702.1. 
Notwithstanding this prohibition, the Commission may provide written advice allowing an official 
to participate under these circumstances if the Commission determines that there are sufficient facts 
to indicate that there will be no reasonably foreseeable measurable impact on the official's property; 
or 
*** 
"( 12) Would cause a reasonably prudent person, using due care and consideration under the 
circumstances, to believe that the governmental decision was of such a nature that its reasonably 
foreseeable effect would influence the market value of the official's property." 
You contend that it is not feasible to develop your property further given how your home is 
situated. Likewise, potential view, privacy, noise levels, and air quality effects arising from the 
ordinances are not likely to have a significant effect on your property given its unique location. 
However, the Hillside ordinances will prevent certain development, and preserve the traditional 
quality of the Hillside neighborhood. Moreover, home buyers might consider neighborhood 
development restrictions to be a disincentive to the purchase of homes in the Hillside neighborhood. 
In this case, the decisions involve property within 500 feet of your residence and there are 
insufficient facts to establish there will be no reasonably foreseeable measurable impact on your 
property. It is also likely that the ordinances limiting certain development in the area would cause a 
reasonably prudent person, using due care and consideration under the circumstances, to believe 
that the governmental decision was of such a nature that its reasonably foreseeable effect would 
influence the market value of your home. Thus, the financial effect of the proposed modifications is 
both foreseeable and material, and you are disqualified from taking part in the decisions except as 
provided below. 


Segmentation and Public Generally: 
File No. A-17-290 
Page No. 4 
Under certain circumstances, a public official disqualified from one decision may participate 
in other related decisions if the official's participation does not affect the decision in which he or she 
has a conflict of interest. (Regulation 18706.) The Commission has advised that an official may 
segment a decision in which the official has a conflict of interest from other decisions in which he 
or she does not have a conflict of interest to allow participation by the official in one or several 
related decisions if the decisions are not inextricably interrelated.3 
You mentioned that decisions regarding the proposed modification to the definition of the 
term "basement" might be bifurcated from the Hillside ordinances. In this event, you may 
participate in decisions regarding the basement ordinance, as long as you strictly adhere to the 
procedure set forth in Regulation 18706. 
Even assuming a foreseeable and material effect on your property, the public generally 
exception permits an official to take part in a decision if the effect of the decision on the official's 
interest is indistinguishable from the effect on the public generally. Regulation 18703 states: 
"A governmental decision's financial effect on a public official's financial interest is 
indistinguishable from its effect on the public generally if the official establishes that a significant 
segment of the public is affected and the effect on his or her financial interest is not unique 
compared to the effect on the significant segment." 
Considering that the proposed modification to the definition of the term "basement" will 
apply city-wide, you have established that a significant segment of the public will be affected. 
Moreover, the proposed modification of the term "basement" would not uniquely affect your 
residence. Thus, you may participate in decisions regarding the basement ordinance, as long as you 
strictly adhere to the segmentation procedure set forth in Regulation 18706 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
RPOC:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Brian G. Lau 
Assistant General Counsel 
ro 
Ryan P. O'Connor 
By: 
Counsel, Legal Division 
1 Decisions are inextricably interrelated if"the result ofone decision will effectively detennine, affirm, nullify, 
or alter the result of another." (Regulation 18 706(b ).)
